title: Informational completeness of qubit measurements and IC preservability of qubit channels: Characterization and Quantification
abstract: Informationally complete (IC) measurements are a useful class of measurements, as their outcome statistics uniquely determine an unknown quantum state. Hence, they are important for certain tasks such as quantum state tomography, quantum process tomography, etc. In this work, we study the quantification of informational completeness for arbitrary quantum measurements by introducing and characterizing a faithful measure for it. We explicitly evaluate the informational completeness of qubit symmetric informationally complete (SIC) measurements and show that it is an upper bound for all qubit minimal informationally complete measurements. Furthermore, by introducing a faithful measure, we try to quantify and characterize the ability of an arbitrary quantum channel to preserve informational completeness of any IC measurement when the channel acts on it in the Heisenberg picture. We call this measure informational completeness-preservability (IC preservability) of quantum channels. After studying its properties, we finally establish its relation to another quantity, namely, the absolute output coherence of a quantum channel, which quantifies the minimum amount of coherence (w.r.t. an arbitrary incoherent basis) that can always be obtained from the output of that channel. Thus, in this work, not only do we try to provide a quantitative framework for studying both the informational completeness of quantum measurements and the ability of quantum channels to preserve it, but we also try to offer key insight into the conceptual relation between informational completeness and quantum coherence.
summary: This paper develops quantitative measures for informational completeness of quantum measurements and the ability of quantum channels to preserve this property. Using qubit systems, the authors establish that SIC measurements saturate the upper bound for IC. Crucially, they derive bounds linking this preservation measure to the channel's absolute output coherence, providing a deep conceptual link between information theory and quantum coherence.
main_problem: The paper aims to quantify the informational completeness (IC) of quantum measurements and characterize the ability of quantum channels to preserve this property (IC preservability).
main_result: The authors establish a quantitative framework showing that the IC of qubit SIC measurements is $1/\sqrt{6}$, and they derive bounds relating IC preservability ($	ilde{D}(\Lambda)$) to the channel's absolute output coherence ($C(\Lambda)$).
method: The work involves defining and characterizing faithful measures for informational completeness, analyzing the action of quantum channels in the Heisenberg picture, and using singular value decompositions of the channel map.
model_or_system: The primary focus is on qubit systems ($d=2$), analyzing general quantum measurements (POVMs) and arbitrary quantum channels ($\Lambda$) acting on these qubits.
key_observables: Informational Completeness ($D(A)$), Informational Completeness-Preservability ($	ilde{D}(\Lambda)$), and Absolute Output Coherence ($C(\Lambda)$).
important_parameters: The dimension $d=2$ (qubits); the singular values ($\lambda_i$) of the channel $\Lambda$.
main_assumptions: The analysis is heavily focused on the qubit case; the derivation of bounds for $	ilde{D}(\Lambda)$ relies on assuming the measurement $A'$ is a qubit SIC measurement.
paper_structure: The paper progresses by first quantifying IC for measurements (establishing the $1/\sqrt{6}$ bound via SIC measurements), then defining and analyzing IC preservability ($	ilde{D}(\Lambda)$) for channels, and finally establishing the relationship between $	ilde{D}(\Lambda)$ and the absolute output coherence $C(\Lambda)$.
why_interesting: This work provides rigorous, quantitative tools to measure how much information a quantum channel preserves about the initial state, linking fundamental concepts like measurement distinguishability (IC) to coherence measures.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
